details.name: Sun (G2V)
details.description: The Sun is a G-type main-sequence star currently in its middle-age phase of a 10-billion-year lifespan. As a G2V star, it functions as a stable nuclear furnace, fusing hydrogen into helium within its core. It will eventually evolve into a Red Giant before shedding its outer layers to become a dense White Dwarf.
key_points: Core Nucleosynthesis: Operates at 15.7 million Kelvin, creating enough thermal pressure to counteract gravitational collapse.
Hydrostatic Equilibrium: Maintains a stable diameter through the perfect balance between outward explosive pressure and inward gravitational pull.
Differential Rotation: Unlike a solid body, the solar plasma rotates faster at the equator (25 days) than at the poles (35 days).
Magnetic Magnetosphere: Generated by the convective movement of plasma, creating the Heliosphere which shields the solar system from cosmic radiation.

details.name: Mercury (Silicate-Iron)
details.description: Mercury is the smallest and innermost planet in the solar system. Classified as a terrestrial planet, it possesses a heavily cratered surface and a high metallic density, suggesting a massive iron core that occupies about 60% of its volume.
key_points: Orbital Eccentricity: Possesses the most elliptical orbit of any planet, causing significant variations in solar distance and orbital velocity.
Exosphere Composition: Lacks a traditional atmosphere, instead maintaining a thin 'exosphere' of atoms blasted off the surface by solar wind.
Thermal Extremes: Experiences the most drastic temperature fluctuations in the solar system, ranging from 430°C in daylight to -180°C at night.
Spin-Orbit Resonance: Completes exactly three rotations on its axis for every two orbits around the Sun, a 3:2 tidal locking ratio.

details.name: Venus (Volcanic-Silicate)
details.description: Often called Earth's sister planet due to similar size and mass, Venus is a terrestrial world encased in a dense, toxic atmosphere. It is the hottest planet in our solar system due to a runaway greenhouse effect.
key_points: Retrograde Rotation: One of only two planets that rotates clockwise on its axis, meaning the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east.
Super-Rotation Atmosphere: High-altitude winds move at 360 km/h, circling the entire planet in just four Earth days, far faster than the planet's slow rotation.
Atmospheric Pressure: The surface pressure is 92 times that of Earth—equivalent to being 900 meters underwater.
Greenhouse Stagnation: Thick clouds of sulfuric acid trap solar radiation, maintaining a global mean surface temperature of approximately 464°C.

details.name: Earth (Lithospheric-Silicate)
details.description: Earth is the only known planet to support life and liquid surface water. It is a geologically active terrestrial world characterized by a dynamic crust and a protective magnetosphere that shields its nitrogen-oxygen atmosphere from solar wind.
key_points: Active Plate Tectonics: The lithosphere is divided into tectonic plates that constantly recycle the crust, regulating global carbon levels and temperatures.
Hydrosphere Equilibrium: Maintains a unique 'Triple Point' environment where water exists simultaneously as gas, liquid, and solid.
Geomagnetic Dynamo: Driven by a liquid outer core of iron and nickel, creating a powerful magnetic field essential for atmospheric retention.
Axial Stability: A relatively large moon stabilizes the planet's axial tilt (23.5°), ensuring predictable seasonal cycles and climate stability.

details.name: Mars (Ferric-Terrestrial)
details.description: Mars is a cold, desert world with a thin atmosphere composed primarily of carbon dioxide. Known as the Red Planet, it features a geologically diverse surface including massive volcanoes, deep canyons, and ancient dried riverbeds that suggest a warmer, wetter past.
key_points: Olympus Mons: Hosts the largest volcano in the solar system, a shield volcano standing nearly three times the height of Mount Everest.
Valles Marineris: Features a vast canyon system stretching over 4,000 km, formed by tectonic cracking of the Martian crust.
Polar Volatiles: Contains permanent ice caps at both poles consisting of water ice and seasonal layers of solid carbon dioxide (dry ice).
Atmospheric Depletion: Lacks a global magnetic field, leading to the gradual stripping of its atmosphere by solar wind over billions of years.

details.name: Jupiter (Gas Giant Jovian)
details.description: Jupiter is the most massive planet in our solar system, acting as a failed star with a composition similar to the Sun. It lacks a solid surface, consisting of deep layers of liquid metallic hydrogen that generate the strongest planetary magnetic field in the system.
key_points: Great Red Spot: An anticyclonic storm larger than Earth that has persisted for at least 350 years, driven by high-velocity zonal winds.
Metallic Hydrogen Layer: Under extreme pressure, hydrogen in the interior becomes electrically conductive, creating a massive magnetosphere.
Gravitational Dominance: Its immense mass acts as a gravitational shield, often intercepting or redirecting comets and asteroids away from the inner solar system.
Trojan Swarms: Orchestrates two massive groups of asteroids (Trojans) that share its orbit, trapped by the balance of Jupiter's and the Sun's gravity.

details.name: Saturn (Gas Giant Ringed)
details.description: Saturn is the second-largest planet and is most famous for its extensive ring system. It is the least dense planet in the solar system—composed mostly of hydrogen and helium—meaning it would technically float if placed in a massive enough body of water.
key_points: Planetary Ring Dynamics: The rings are composed of billions of individual ice and rock particles, ranging from dust-sized grains to mountain-sized boulders.
Hexagonal Polar Jet: Features a persistent, six-sided jet stream pattern at its north pole, a unique fluid dynamics phenomenon not seen elsewhere in the solar system.
Extreme Oblateness: Because it rotates so rapidly and has a low density, the planet is noticeably flattened at its poles and bulges at its equator.
Acoustic Vibrations: The planet's interior oscillates constantly, creating waves in the rings that scientists use like a seismograph to study the planet's core.

details.name: Uranus (Methane Rich)
details.description: Uranus is an ice giant that orbits the Sun on its side, a unique orientation in the solar system. Its pale cyan color is the result of methane gas in its atmosphere absorbing red light, while its interior consists of a hot, dense 'icy' fluid over a rocky core.
key_points: Extreme Axial Tilt: Rotates at a nearly 98-degree angle, likely caused by a massive collision, resulting in extreme seasonal variations lasting 21 years.
Tropospheric Composition: The atmosphere contains the coldest planetary temperatures recorded (49 K), with clouds formed of hydrogen sulfide.
Asymmetric Magnetosphere: Unlike most planets, its magnetic field is tilted 59 degrees away from its rotation axis and is offset from the planet's center.
Internal Heat Deficit: Radiates very little heat into space compared to other giants, suggesting its internal heat may have been lost during its ancient impact event.

details.name: Neptune (Ice Giant)
details.description: Neptune is the most distant major planet in our solar system, characterized by its deep blue hue and violent weather patterns. It is an ice giant with a mantle of water, ammonia, and methane ice surrounding a core of rock and ice.
key_points: Supersonic Winds: Features the fastest planetary winds in the solar system, reaching speeds of over 2,100 km/h—nearly five times the speed of sound.
Internal Heat Source: Despite its distance from the Sun, it radiates 2.6 times more energy than it receives, likely due to leftover heat from its formation and gravitational contraction.
Transient Storm Systems: Known for large, dark anticyclonic storms (like the Great Dark Spot) that appear and disappear within its high-altitude methane clouds.
Triton’s Retrograde Orbit: Neptune's largest moon orbits in the opposite direction of the planet's rotation, indicating it was likely a Kuiper Belt object captured by Neptune’s gravity.

details.name: Pluto (Ice-Dwarf)
details.description: Pluto is a complex world of ice mountains and frozen plains. Once considered the ninth planet, it is now the most famous dwarf planet, possessing a thin atmosphere and a geologically young surface shaped by flowing nitrogen glaciers.
key_points: Sputnik Planitia: A massive, heart-shaped glacier made of nitrogen and carbon monoxide ice that undergoes constant convection, refreshing the surface.
Binary System Dynamics: Pluto's largest moon, Charon, is so massive that the two bodies actually orbit a common center of mass located in the space between them.
Atmospheric Haze: When Pluto is closer to the Sun, its surface ices sublimate to create a blue-tinted atmosphere with distinct layers of hydrocarbon haze.
Highly Elliptical Orbit: Its path is so eccentric that it occasionally crosses inside Neptune's orbit, bringing it closer to the Sun than the eighth planet for 20-year periods.
